Why: Each manuscript value should trace to a source output or listing.
Evidence: TLF number, listing, analysis dataset, output path.

Why: Incorrect denominators or rounding are common manuscript QC issues.
Evidence: n/N values, table denominators, recalculated percentages.

Why: Means, SDs, medians, ranges, and IQRs can be transcribed incorrectly.

Check: CI, p-value, and effect estimate verification
Why: P-values, CIs, HRs, RRs, ORs, and differences drive conclusions.
KM estimates, events, censoring, HRs, and median follow-up must align.
Evidence: KM tables, censoring tables, survival figures.

Why: Safety denominators and exposure-adjusted rates are often inconsistent.
Evidence: Safety population, exposure tables, AE summaries.

Why: Abstract, text, tables, figures, supplements, CSR, and registry should align.
Unit or scale errors can create clinically meaningful inaccuracies.
Inconsistent precision distracts and can imply false accuracy.
Evidence: Style guide, TLF shells, journal rules.

Why: Scientific numbers should use one defined style across the document.
Evidence: Style guide and examples in manuscript.

Why: Values such as .25 or inconsistent trailing zeros should follow style rules.
Avoid formats such as p = 0.000; use appropriate thresholds and precision.
Evidence: Style guide, statistical convention, output p-values.

Why: CI notation, separators, brackets, and decimal places should be consistent.
Percent signs, rate units, ratio labels, and denominator footnotes should be clear.
Evidence: TLF shells, manuscript tables, figure legends.

Why: Missing symbols, N/A, NE, NR, and NA need consistent definitions.
PDF/Word rendering can introduce line breaks, missing symbols, or table misalignment.
